examples.title: Basic Event Handler Registration
examples.description: Demonstrates how to register and use event handlers in JavaScript
examples.explanation: Shows two ways to register event listeners: using a named function and using an arrow function. The event object contains details about the triggered event.
examples.timeComplexity: Time: O(1) per event, Space: O(n) for listeners

examples.title: Java Event Handling with ActionListener
examples.description: Demonstrates event handling in Java GUI applications using ActionListener interface
examples.explanation: Java uses the ActionListener interface with actionPerformed method. The component must be registered using addActionListener(). When button is clicked, actionPerformed is invoked.
examples.timeComplexity: Time: O(1) per event, Space: O(1)

examples.title: C# Event Handling with Delegates
examples.description: Demonstrates event handling in C# using delegates and the event keyword
examples.explanation: C# uses delegates for type-safe event handling. The event keyword restricts external access to += and -= operators only. Delegates can point to multiple methods (multicast).
examples.timeComplexity: Time: O(n) for multicast delegates, Space: O(n)

examples.title: Python Event Handling with Tkinter
examples.description: Demonstrates GUI event handling in Python using Tkinter
examples.code:
import tkinter as tk
from tkinter import messagebox

def on_button_click(event):
 messagebox.showinfo("Event", f"Button clicked!\nEvent type: {event.type}")
 entry_text.set(entry.get() + "*")

def on_key_press(event):
 print(f"Key pressed: {event.char}")

def on_mouse_move(event):
 label.config(text=f"Mouse: {event.x}, {event.y}")

# Create window
root = tk.Tk()
root.title("Event Handling Demo")

# StringVar for dynamic text
entry_text = tk.StringVar()

# GUI Components
entry = tk.Entry(root, textvariable=entry_text)
entry.pack(pady=5)

button = tk.Button(root, text="Click Me")
button.pack(pady=5)

label = tk.Label(root, text="Move mouse here")
label.pack(pady=5)

# Bind events
button.bind("<Button-1>", on_button_click) # Mouse click
entry.bind("<KeyPress>", on_key_press) # Keyboard
label.bind("<Motion>", on_mouse_move) # Mouse move

root.mainloop()
examples.explanation: Python Tkinter uses bind() method to associate events with handler functions. Events are specified as strings like '<Button-1>' for mouse click. The event object contains detailed information.
examples.timeComplexity: Time: O(1) per event, Space: O(n) for bindings
